skill.name: gitlab-ci-patterns
skill.description: Build GitLab CI/CD pipelines with multi-stage workflows, caching, security scanning, Docker builds, and deployment automation patterns.
skill.summary: Create GitLab CI/CD pipeline designs for testing, builds, deployments, caching, scanning, and runner-based automation.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly false positives because they appear inside GitLab CI YAML examples, not executable skill code. No prompt injection or malicious data exfiltration was found, but some examples recommend insecure deployment practices that require user review.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Kubernetes TLS Verification in Example
medium_findings.description: The deployment template sets kubectl with --insecure-skip-tls-verify=true. Users who copy this pattern could accept spoofed Kubernetes API servers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The insecure TLS flag is explicit in a deployment example. It is documentation rather than executable skill code, so the risk depends on user adoption.

medium_findings.title: Docker Registry Password Passed on Command Line
medium_findings.description: The Docker login example passes $CI_REGISTRY_PASSWORD with -p. This can expose secrets through process listings or logs in some CI environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The password flag is clearly shown in the sample. GitLab masking reduces some exposure, but safer stdin-based login should be recommended.

medium_findings.title: Terraform Auto-Approve Deployment Pattern
medium_findings.description: The Terraform apply job uses -auto-approve. A manual gate lowers risk, but copied pipelines could apply infrastructure changes without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is real and can be risky in infrastructure pipelines. Context includes a manual trigger, so this is a moderate configuration concern.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are CI Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur inside fenced YAML examples. They are not executed by the skill and show ordinary CI job commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every command match is within Markdown code fences for GitLab CI configuration. No repository script or runtime hook executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholder Environment URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs use example.com hostnames for GitLab environment metadata. No outbound request logic or data exfiltration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are reserved example domains used as deployment labels. They are not called by any executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to descriptive text and related skill names. No cryptographic algorithm use was present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain GitLab CI wording and a related skill name. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Disabling TLS Verification
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample Kubernetes setup disables TLS certificate verification. This should be replaced with validated cluster certificate configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact insecure flag is visible on the kubectl configuration line. The finding is limited to documentation guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Secret Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The Docker login example passes a password through a command option. A password-stdin pattern is safer for registry authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret variable is used directly with the -p option. The sample is legitimate CI guidance but should be hardened.

content.value_statement: GitLab CI pipelines can become slow, inconsistent, and hard to maintain. This skill provides reusable patterns for stages, caching, deployments, and scanning.
actual_capabilities: Designs multi-stage GitLab CI pipelines for build, test, and deploy workflows.
Provides Docker image build and registry push patterns for GitLab projects.
Shows Kubernetes deployment jobs with staging and production environments.
Outlines Terraform validation, planning, and manual apply pipeline stages.
Includes security scanning, caching, artifact, and child pipeline examples.
limitations: It provides patterns, not a complete project-specific pipeline generator.
It does not validate GitLab syntax against a live GitLab instance.
Deployment examples require secure variables and environment-specific review.
Some examples need hardening before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a New Pipeline
use_cases.description: Set up a GitLab CI workflow with separate build, test, and deploy stages.

use_cases.title: Standardize Deployment Jobs
use_cases.description: Use shared deployment templates for staging and production Kubernetes releases.

use_cases.title: Improve Pipeline Speed
use_cases.description: Apply caching, artifacts, and child pipeline patterns to reduce repeated work.

prompt_templates.title: Basic GitLab Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitLab CI pipeline for my project with build, test, and deploy stages. Explain each stage in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Docker Build Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitLab CI workflow that builds a Docker image, tags it by commit, and pushes it to the GitLab registry.

prompt_templates.title: Environment Deployments
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitLab CI deployment pattern for staging on develop and production on main with manual approval.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Enterprise Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GitLab CI design for caching, artifacts, child pipelines, Terraform stages, and security scanning coverage.

output_examples.input: I need a GitLab CI pipeline for a Node.js service.
output_examples.output: A staged plan with dependency installation, build artifacts, linting, tests, coverage reporting, and a main-branch deployment gate.

output_examples.input: Our deployments need staging and production separation.
output_examples.output: A deployment structure with shared job settings, namespace-specific rollout checks, branch rules, and manual production approval.

output_examples.input: Our pipeline is slow and repeats the same install steps.
output_examples.output: A caching and artifact strategy that separates dependency cache keys, build outputs, and downstream job dependencies.

best_practices: Use pinned container image versions and review every privileged service.
Store secrets in protected GitLab CI variables with masking enabled.
Keep production deployment jobs manual and tied to protected branches.
anti_patterns: Do not copy deployment examples without replacing placeholder URLs and names.
Do not disable TLS verification for production Kubernetes clusters.
Do not pass registry passwords through command-line flags when safer options exist.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete GitLab CI files?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples that can be adapted into complete GitLab CI files.

faq.question: Can it help with Docker image publishing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Docker build, tag, login, and push workflow patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover Kubernetes deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows staging and production deployment structures with rollout checks.

faq.question: Can it improve pipeline performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes cache, artifact, and child pipeline patterns for common bottlenecks.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: Use it as guidance and harden secrets, TLS settings, approvals, and branch protections before production.

faq.question: Does it require GitLab network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill content is documentation and does not connect to GitLab by itself.
